Barbarlanj 
Description: This is not the 
barbarian of history. but the bar· 
barian of fantasy fiction. He's a 
powerful warrior from a culture 
on the fringes of civilization. 
He's left his home to sell his 
skills and adventure In the civilized world-perhaps to amass a 
fortune with which to return 
home. perhaps to become an im· 
portant figure In this so-called 
clvlUzaUon. He's known for 
strength. cunning. contempt for 
the outer world's decadence. 
and for adhering to his own code 
of honor. 
The barbarian Is usually very 
strong: therefore, the barbarian 
must have a Strength abWty 
scare or 15 or more. A character 
can come from a barbarian tribe 
and have a lower Strength than 
that-but he cannot have the 
Barbarian Kit.

Role: The typical RPG barbarian is a powerful. dangerous figure. as though he were an 
animal totem in human skin. In 
a campaign, he's a front-line 
fighter with some special skiJJs 
and a very different outlook 
than the rest of the characters; 
his player should always play 
him as someone from a different 
land, someone whose likes and 
dislikes and perceptions are 
based on a different culture. (If 
you play him as Just another 
warrior (rom down the street, 
you lose a lot o( the mystique 
the character has.) 
If the PC party has no real 
leader, he may gravitate to that 
role; i( It has a good enough 
leader. he'll probably stick to beIng a specialist In the things he 
does well, 
Secondary Skills: The OM 
wHi decide. based on the character's background. what sort o( 
secondary skill would be required, Most barbarian tribes 
have a required skl1J; a tribe that 
makes ils living by fishing 
would have Fisher as its requIred secondary skill. 
Weapon Proficiencies: Required: Batt.le Axe, Bastard 
Sword. (These are the classical 
fiction-barbarian weapons: the 
OM may decide to substitute 
others more appropriate to his 
own world,) Barbarian figh ters 
may specialize In any weapon. 
but are not likely to encounter 
unusual weapons [like lances. 
quarterstaves, naIls, peculiar 
polearms} until they reach the 
ou ter world. Recommended: 
Bow (any), Sling, Sword (anyl. 
War Hammer. 
Nonweapon Proficiencies: 
Bonus Proficiency: Endurance. 
Recommended: General-Animal Handling. Animal Training. 
Direction Sense. Fire-Building. 
Riding (Land-Based). Weather 
Sense. (Warrior) BUnd-f lghtIng, 
Hunting. Mountaineering. Running. Set Snares, Survival. 
Tracking. (Priest- costs twice 
the lis ted number o( slots if 
Fighter or Ranger. or Just the 
listed number if Paladin) Herbalism. (Rogue-costs double 
slots) Jumping. The OM is 
withIn his rights to Insist that 
the Barbarian character take a 
proficiency in the tribal speclall}, (Fishing. Agriculture. 
whatever) if the OM so wishes. 
Equipment: The character. 
when he spends his starling 
gold . may not buy armor 
heavier 
[han 
SpliOl 
mail, 
banded mail, or bronze plate 
mail. Outside his tribe. once he 
has adventured In the outer 
world, he can usc any type ofarmar without penalty. When he 
spends his starting gold. he 
must limit hlmsel( to weapons 
the OM says arc appropriatc for 
his tribe-the usual group of 
weapons Includes battle axe. 
bows (any). club. dagger or dirk. 
(ootman's nail. mace, or pick, 
hand or throwing axe. sUng, 
spear, or sword (any). 
Special Benefits: Barbarians 
are Impressive because of sheer 
strength, intensity. and animal 
magnellsm; this gives them a 
+3 reaction adjustment bonus 
In certain situations. 
Whenever the barbarian character achlevcsa reaclion roll 0(8 
or less (including Charisma and 
racial bonuses). you subtract 
the modifier. That is. I(!.he reaction is positive at all. It will be 
even more positive than it otherwise would have been, 
Example: Thralh the Thranaran is a Barbarian with a Charism a of 15, Encountering a 
knighr who could be friend or 
foc lO him, he speaks wUh the 
knight In a friendly fashion. The 
DM rolls his Encounter Reaction 
and achieves an lI on 2d I O. On 
the "FrieJldly" column of lile 
Encoum er Reactions chart from 
the Dungeon Master's Guide, 
this is a "Cautious" reaction. 
Bur wail-llfs charisma gives 
him a +3 bonus. The 11 becomes an 8, sWI an IndlffereOl 
11 
reaction. Bu r because he's 
reached an 8, his Bar barian b0nus comes inca pJaJ~ making the 
final react,jon roll a 5: A friendly 
reaction. 
Special Hindrances: All that 
im pressiveness 
can 
work 
against the Barbarian . too. 
Whenever the barbarian char· 
acter achieves a reaction roll o( 
14 or more. he takes an additionaJ - 3 modifier. That Is. if the 
reaction is negative at all. It will 
be even more negaHve than it 
otherwise would have beenthe barbarian is scary, and the 
other person overreacts. 
Exanlple: Thrath next m eets a 
suspicious witch, and is indifferent toward her. On the " Indifferent " column of the Encounter 
Reactions chart. t/Je OM rolls a 
17, 1brath 's Charisma bonus of 
3reduees the roll loa 14, butHs 
still enough that his Barbarian 
penally Just shoots il right back 
up to a 17 agaln, The witcll becomes Threatening. 
Wealth Options: The BarbarIan gelS the starling gold (or a 
Warrior (5d4x 10 gp), but he 
muslspend Itall (be(ore starting 
play) except three gp or less: he 
can have some pocket change 
when he reaches civilization. 
but must be close to penniless, 
Races: Oemihuman Barbarians (allow the same rules. 
Dwarves are perhaps the most 
admirably suited to belngBarbarlans_ The OM will have to decide 
whether his elves. half-elves and 
gnomes are brtX>dlng and menacIng enough to be Barbarians: the 
question is even harder with the 
leisure-loving halOings. But if the 
OM wishes to allow any or all of 
these demihuman races to have 
Barbarians among them. he may. 
Final Note: Most classic 
fantasy-fiction barbarians are 
male_ but this Warrior Kit can 
certainly be laken by (emale 
characters. with all the Kit's requirements. benefits, and hindrances In effect.

Beast·Rlder 
Description: The Beast·Rlder 
Is a warrior In a tribe or clan 
(usua lly a barbarian tribe) 
which has a strong affinity for 
one type of animal. The animal 
Is the tOlem of the Uibe. and the 
Beast-Rider makes friends very 
easily with that type of anImal 
and can uaJn Il Imo a riding· 
beast . .. even {flt'sa rype of anlmaJ not normally considered a 
riding-beast. 
In a campaign. the Beastruder Is an exotic warrior who Is 
notable for his kinship with his 
a nimal: like the Barbarian. he 
brings a wlld, oULSider'S3(Utude 
InlO the adventuring party. His 
animaJ also has abilities which 
can benefit the adven turing 
party. However. the morc unusual lhe anlmaJ Is, the harder It 
Is to accommodate In all situations: It's no problem to stable a 
horse althe inn. butjusl try stabling a grea t white wolf. a wUd 
boar. or a dolphin! 
1b be a Beast-Rider. the char· 
acter must ha ve a Charisma of 
alleasl 13. (Naturally. there a rc 
members of lhc Beast-Rider's 
tribe who are not themselves 
Beast·Riders; the Beast-Riders 
a re lhe tribe's elite warriors.) 
Role: As mentloncd. in his 
own SOCiety. the Beast·Rider Is 
the elite warnor. and he com· 
mands a lot of respect among 
his own kind. Outside his LTlbal 
grounds. however. he's very definitely an outsider. His barbarIa n 
mannerisms 
a nd 
his 
obvious and very unusual 
friendship with his animal set 
him apart from most socleUes. 
Because of this, the Beast·Rlder 
may become especially attached to the other playercharacters (If they treat him as 
an equal and not a freak). even If 
he'd ne\'er admit ilIa them. 
The DM needs to re,inrorce this 
social role by having NPCs react 
to the Beast-Rider's strange· 
ness. For Inslance. NPCs will be 
leery or speaking to or negotiating with the Beast·Rlder If 
there's a more "civilized" char· 
acter on hand to perform those 
functions. The OM needs constantly to use the Beast-Rldcr's 
reaction modifiers. listed below 
under '·hindrances."
